signals.idea: Allow non-admin community members to message the BNDC Discord bot directly, with access controls. Key requirements: 1) Add a flag on the members table (e.g. can_message_bot) that controls who can interact with the bot. 2) Cordon off sensitive operations so non-admin users can only do safe read-only things (search messages, look up member info, etc.) while admins retain full control (delete messages, send messages, manage server, etc.). 3) Figure out what data/operations are actually sensitive and should be restricted. The bot currently only works via admin_chat - need to understand the full current architecture and design how to open it up safely.

unresolved_flags.concern: The plan opens non-admin DMs in `AdminChatCog` but does not account for the existing DM listener in `AdminCog`. As written, approved non-admin users would still get the fallback robot-sounds DM, so the two cogs would conflict on the same message path.

The plan frames access control mostly as tool filtering, but the current repo also leaks channel data through prompt construction. If you allow public @mentions, the bot already injects the replied-to message and the last 10 channel messages into the LLM prompt before any tool call, and member-safe tools can still fetch live channel/message content by ID. The plan needs an explicit policy for which channels/contexts non-admins may invoke the bot from.

The plan puts `can_message_bot` on the global `members` table, but the repository has already moved core bot configuration to guild scope. That makes the proposed access model fight the current architecture and bakes in another migration if per-guild enablement becomes necessary.

The plan keeps `query_table` for non-admins, but in this repo that tool is effectively raw database access over a broad allowlist. Restricting a few table names is not enough: `members` now contains `auth_user_id`, and `query_table` can return arbitrary columns from `members`, `grant_applications`, `invite_codes`, `pending_intros`, and `timed_mutes`. For member access, this should be removed or replaced with a strict table-and-column allowlist.

signals.loop_summary: Iteration 1. Weighted score trajectory: 8.5. Plan deltas: n/a. Recurring critiques: 0. Resolved flags: 0. Open significant flags: 4.

items: Non-admin users with `can_message_bot=True` can DM the bot and receive responses using read-only tools

Non-admin users cannot invoke write tools (send_message, delete_message, edit_message, upload_file, share_to_social) — blocked at both prompt level (tools not offered to Claude) and executor level (rejected if somehow called)
Admin users experience zero regressions — all 16 tools remain available
Rate limiting prevents non-admin users from excessive API usage
search_logs and other sensitive read operations are restricted to admins
The `can_message_bot` flag can be toggled per-member via the database (and eventually via admin bot commands)

unresolved_flags.evidence: `src/features/admin/admin_cog.py:295-316` replies to every non-admin DM; `src/features/admin_chat/admin_chat_cog.py:99-107` is the gate the plan intends to relax for those same DMs.

unresolved_flags.concern: The plan frames access control mostly as tool filtering, but the current repo also leaks channel data through prompt construction. If you allow public @mentions, the bot already injects the replied-to message and the last 10 channel messages into the LLM prompt before any tool call, and member-safe tools can still fetch live channel/message content by ID. The plan needs an explicit policy for which channels/contexts non-admins may invoke the bot from.
unresolved_flags.evidence: `src/features/admin_chat/admin_chat_cog.py:136-169` builds `channel_context` with replied-to content and recent messages; `src/features/admin_chat/agent.py:136-157` injects that context into the prompt; `src/features/admin_chat/tools.py:559-567` and `src/features/admin_chat/tools.py:743-759` fetch live Discord data for `find_messages(live=true)` and `inspect_message`.

unresolved_flags.concern: The plan puts `can_message_bot` on the global `members` table, but the repository has already moved core bot configuration to guild scope. That makes the proposed access model fight the current architecture and bakes in another migration if per-guild enablement becomes necessary.
unresolved_flags.evidence: `supabase/migrations/20260320000000_multi_server.sql:7-25` adds `server_config.admin_user_id`; `supabase/migrations/20260320000000_multi_server.sql:84-93` adds `guild_members`; `src/common/server_config.py:26-106` resolves runtime behavior per guild; meanwhile `src/features/admin_chat/admin_chat_cog.py:32-41` still uses env `ADMIN_USER_ID`.

unresolved_flags.evidence: `src/features/admin_chat/tools.py:64-70` includes `members`, `invite_codes`, `grant_applications`, `pending_intros`, and `timed_mutes` in `QUERYABLE_TABLES`; `src/features/admin_chat/tools.py:1313-1380` allows arbitrary `select`/filters/order on those tables; `supabase/migrations/20260324100000_redesign_for_elegance.sql:8-28` adds `members.auth_user_id`.
